interactions.summary: Empty or boundary-damaged segment after a student reading; no semantic content.

Jiang develops the Dido paradox: Virgil names countless shades but refuses to name Dido, the one figure he understands best, because she is his own realistic creation and his treatment of her exposes guilt, resentment, and unfair condemnation.
A student reads Dante asking to speak with the lovers, with the text explicitly naming the place where Dido suffers.

Jiang reads Dante's naming of Dido as rebellion against Virgil and an act of memory-resurrection, then closes by saying Inferno requires heart, imagination, instinct, intuition, and faith because hell is illusion and deception.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 is quoted Dante material, not an audience question.
Jiang asks 'Any questions?' at the end, but no audience question is captured in this source.

claims.claim: The Dido paradox is that Virgil names many shades but refuses to name the one he knows most intimately: Dido, his own creation.
Jiang contrasts Virgil condemning Dido to hell with Dante elevating Beatrice to heaven.
Jiang speculates that Dido feels realistic because she is based on someone Virgil knew and perhaps loved, making his condemnation of her a guilty act of hatred.
Dante naming Dido is an act of rebellion against Virgil as father, guide, and teacher because Dante refuses to inherit Virgil's erasure.
Naming Dido resurrects her in memory and shows that it was wrong for Virgil both to condemn her and then ignore her.
The Divine Comedy requires heart, imagination, instinct, intuition, and faith because the truth of Inferno cannot be known by surface reading alone.

signature_moments.moment: Virgil names a thousand shades but cannot name the one person he knows best.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the person that Virgil refuses to acknowledge by name is the one that he understands the best
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns omission into confession: the missing name is the evidence.

signature_moments.moment: Virgil condemns Dido to hell; Dante elevates Beatrice to heaven.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Virgil condemned Dido to hell. Dante elevated Beatrice to heaven.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The whole rivalry between Virgilian hatred and Dantean love appears in one contrast.

signature_moments.moment: Dante names Dido to resurrect her in memory after Virgil tried to erase her.
signature_moments.source_phrase: name her in order to resurrect her in our memory
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes interpretation an ethical act: remembering repairs an authority's erasure.

signature_moments.moment: The Divine Comedy must be seen with the heart and imagination, because hell is illusion and deception.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You have to see with your heart. You have to see with your imagination.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It closes the lecture by making intuition and faith part of the reading method.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name-like problem: Virgil names many shades but refuses to name Dido, the person his own poem most intimately created.
Dante naming Dido as a way to restore a person an authority tried to erase.
The method Jiang says The Divine Comedy requires because surface reading cannot reveal hell's deceptions.

chronology_notes.note: The 2026-04-08 episode ends as the first Dante session, with Jiang saying the class will continue next time.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript says 'Iliad' where context indicates Aeneid; Dido is a Virgil/Aeneid figure.
Transcript renders 'Dante' as 'Donna' in one sentence; semantic extraction treats it as Dante.
